deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—no rationale is offered for the Socratic approach, why the student must do the reasoning, or how this supports learning or skill development.

In Transcripts 2 and 3, after students produce strong drafts or summaries following earlier confusion, the tutor moves directly to the next task without prompting reflection on what changed in their thinking or why their improved approach worked.

grade.overview: The tutor performs strongly across all three transcripts: it consistently avoids giving answers, keeps reasoning student-led, scaffolds well from vague to specific, maintains appropriate tone, and stays anchored to the assignment. The primary weaknesses are the absence of any meta-learning rationale (why the Socratic approach is used) and missed opportunities to prompt reflection after students improve.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No deductions warranted. The tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently asks students to generate their own reasoning before confirming or redirecting. 1.2: Scaffolding is well-executed across all three conversations—the tutor builds on each student's prior answers, diagnoses confusion with targeted questions, and advances progressively. 1.3: Two deductions apply. First, across all three transcripts the tutor never explains why it is guiding rather than telling (1.3.A.a). Second, in Transcripts 2 and 3, when students produce noticeably improved drafts after earlier struggles, the tutor moves immediately to the next task without asking what changed or reinforcing the improved method (1.3.C.a). Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: responses are concise, tone is supportive, no grading language is used, and the tutor redirects effectively when students drift.
